---
title: "Lab 2"
subtitle: "Confidence Intervals and A/A Validation for an A/B Experiment"
author: "Ihor Miroshnychenko"
institute: Kyiv School of Economics
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    number-sections: true
    highlight-style: github
    code-line-numbers: false
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
---

# Objective

In **Lab 1** you designed a one-sample A/B experiment for your own
business case and walked through the basic test mechanics: hypotheses,
critical region, $p$-value, power, and MDE.

In **Lab 2** you take the **same business case** and go beyond a
single accept/reject decision. The focus of this lab is the
**confidence interval** --- the object that actually drives product
decisions in practice, because it tells the stakeholder *how much* the
metric could have moved, not just *whether* it moved.

You will:

1. Wrap your test logic into a reusable `my_binom_test()` function that
   returns a full structured result (estimate, $p$-value, CI, decision).
2. Build confidence intervals **by several methods**, compare them, and
   verify that they actually deliver the promised coverage.
3. Run an **A/A test** to confirm that your testing infrastructure is
   correctly calibrated before any real launch.
4. Combine all of the above into a single stakeholder recommendation.

# General Requirements

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
## Continuity with Lab 1

- You must reuse the **same business case** from Lab 1 (same product,
  same metric, same baseline $\mu_0$). Inventing a brand-new case is
  not allowed.
- If your Lab 1 case had methodological issues, fix them in
  @sec-recap and explain what you changed.
- Copying another student's case (whether from Lab 1 or Lab 2) is still
  prohibited.
:::

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Report Format

Submit the report in **one** of the following formats:

- **Quarto → PDF** (`.qmd` → `.pdf`)
- **Quarto → HTML** (`.qmd` → `.html`, hosted separately, e.g. on
  [Quarto Pub](https://quartopub.com/), [Posit Connect Cloud](https://connect.posit.cloud/), etc.)
- **Jupyter Notebook** (`.ipynb`)
- **Google Colab** (share the link with viewing permissions)

The report must contain both code **and** narrative text. Every section
begins with a written explanation, not code.
:::

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## Restrictions on libraries

You may use `numpy`, `pandas`, `matplotlib`, `scipy.stats.binom`, and
`statsmodels.stats.proportion.proportion_confint`. You **may not** use
`scipy.stats.binomtest`, `statsmodels.stats.proportion.binom_test`, or
any other ready-made hypothesis-test function except as a verification
step. The core machinery must be your own code.
:::

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## Deadline

📅 **May 27, 2026, 23:59**
:::

# Tasks

## Recap of the Lab 1 Case {#sec-recap}

::: {.border}
1. In one paragraph, restate the business problem, the success metric,
   and the baseline $\mu_0$.
2. State the hypotheses, the chosen significance level $\alpha$, and the
   business MDE (with its monetary justification from Lab 1).
3. Briefly summarise what you found in Lab 1: the planned sample size
   $N$, the observed $q$, the $p$-value, and your decision.
4. If you discovered a methodological issue in Lab 1 (wrong
   alternative, misjudged MDE, etc.), state the fix here and use the
   corrected numbers throughout Lab 2.
:::

## A Reusable `my_binom_test()` Function {#sec-mybinom}

::: {.border}
1. Implement a function `my_binom_test(q, n, mu0, alternative, alpha, conf_int)`
   with the following contract:
   - Supports `alternative` $\in$ `{"greater", "less", "two-sided"}`.
     For the two-sided case use $p = 2 \cdot \min(p_{\text{left}}, p_{\text{right}})$.
   - When `conf_int=True`, also returns a confidence interval built by
     **inverting the test** (the same algorithm shown in the lecture
     and Practice 2).
   - Returns a **dict** with at least: `estimate`, `p_value`, `reject`,
     `ci_low`, `ci_high`.
2. Apply the function to your Lab 1 observation $q$. Print the full
   result for the alternative your business actually cares about.
3. **Verify your implementation** against `scipy.stats.binomtest` (this
   is the only legitimate use of the built-in function). Show that
   $p$-values match to at least 4 decimal places. If the CI differs
   slightly from `binomtest.proportion_ci`, explain why (which
   construction does each one use?).
:::

## Confidence Intervals: Construction {#sec-ci-build}

::: {.border}
1. For your observed $q$ and $n$, compute a $95\%$ confidence interval
   using **four** different constructions:
   - **Inversion** (your own `my_binom_test`, two-sided).
   - **Clopper–Pearson** (exact) via
     `proportion_confint(method="beta")`.
   - **Wilson** via `proportion_confint(method="wilson")`.
   - **Normal (Wald) approximation** via
     `proportion_confint(method="normal")`.
2. Put all four intervals into a small table with columns
   `method | lower | upper | width`.
3. Comment on the differences. In particular:
   - Which intervals are guaranteed to lie inside $[0, 1]$, and which
     can produce nonsense bounds for small $n$ or extreme $\hat{\mu}$?
   - Why is the Wald interval almost always the **narrowest**, and
     why is that not a good reason to prefer it?
   - Which two methods coincide for moderate $n$ and $\hat{\mu}$ near
     $0.5$?
4. **One-sided lower bound.** Using your `my_binom_test` with
   `alternative` set to the side your business cares about, compute the
   one-sided $95\%$ bound. Verify the lecture equivalence: the
   one-sided lower bound at level $1 - \alpha$ equals the lower edge of
   the **two-sided** interval at level $1 - 2\alpha$. Show both numbers
   side by side.
:::

## Confidence Intervals: Properties and Interpretation {#sec-ci-props}

::: {.border}
1. **Coverage simulation.** Pick a "true" value `latent_mu` for your
   metric (you may use the value from Lab 1) and your sample size $N$.
   Run $\geq 5\,000$ independent simulated experiments under this
   `latent_mu`. For each one, build a $95\%$ confidence interval using
   *either* your inversion-based interval *or* Wilson. Report the
   empirical **coverage probability** (the fraction of intervals that
   contain `latent_mu`). It should be $\geq 0.95$. Why is the actual
   coverage usually slightly **above** $0.95$ for the binomial?
2. **CI width vs. sample size.** Holding $\hat{\mu} = q/n$ constant at
   your Lab 1 value, plot the **Wilson CI width** as a function of $N$
   for $N$ from a small value up to $\geq 5N_{\text{plan}}$. On the
   plot, mark:
   - The Lab 1 planned $N$.
   - The $N$ at which the CI shrinks to a width your business
     considers "tight enough" --- and explain that threshold in
     monetary terms (one paragraph).
3. **Decision rule from a CI.** Suppose your business agreed on a
   threshold $\mu^{*}$ above which the new variant gets shipped (e.g.
   $\mu^{*} = \mu_0 + \text{business MDE}$). Translate the following
   three outcomes into ship/iterate/extend decisions, and justify each:
   - Lower CI bound is $\geq \mu^{*}$.
   - CI contains both $\mu_0$ and $\mu^{*}$.
   - Lower CI bound is between $\mu_0$ and $\mu^{*}$.
4. **Common pitfalls.** In one short paragraph, state in plain English:
   - What the $95\%$ confidence level **does** mean.
   - What it **does not** mean (the "probability of the parameter"
     trap). Why is this distinction more than pedantic --- give a
     scenario in which the wrong interpretation would lead to a wrong
     decision.
:::

## BONUS: A/A Test: Validating Your Infrastructure {#sec-aa}

Before any real A/B test goes to production, the standard sanity check
is an **A/A test**: split users into two groups that get the *same*
experience and confirm that the test rejects $H_0$ no more often than
$\alpha$. If it does, the testing platform itself is broken (bad
randomisation, leaked events, sample-ratio mismatch, ...) and any
subsequent A/B result is meaningless.

::: {.border}
1. Run **$\geq 5\,000$** simulated A/A experiments under $H_0$ with
   your sample size $N$ and your baseline $\mu_0$. For each one,
   compute the $p$-value using your `my_binom_test`.
2. Plot the **histogram of $p$-values**. Comment on its shape:
   - Why is it expected to be approximately uniform on $[0, 1]$?
   - Why does the binomial give a slightly "stepped" pattern, and what
     would a *broken* infrastructure histogram look like (sketch the
     shape verbally)?
3. Report the **empirical FPR** (fraction of $p \leq \alpha$). Compute
   a $95\%$ Wilson CI around this empirical FPR. Is the nominal
   $\alpha$ inside it? Use this to argue, with simulation evidence,
   that your test is correctly calibrated.
4. **Two product-specific failure modes.** Describe two realistic ways
   a real A/A test could fail *for your particular product* (not
   generic "data is wrong"). Examples of the right level of
   specificity: "the same household IP appears in both groups", "the
   tutorial event is logged once for re-installers and counted twice",
   "weekend traffic is exclusively routed to group A". For each
   failure mode, predict what the $p$-value histogram would look like.
:::

## Final Recommendation {#sec-final}

::: {.border}
Putting all of the above together, write a **single-page
recommendation memo** (≤ 400 words) addressed to a non-technical
stakeholder. It must contain:

1. The observed point estimate, the chosen $95\%$ confidence interval
   (state which construction and why), and the one-sided lower bound.
2. The decision (ship / iterate / extend the test) and the **reason**
   behind it, framed in terms of the CI and the business threshold
   $\mu^{*}$ --- not just the $p$-value.
3. One honest caveat: did the A/A check pass cleanly, or are there
   open questions about the infrastructure?

The memo must be readable by someone who has never heard the word
"p-value". You will be graded on whether a real product manager could
act on it.
:::

# Grading {.unnumbered}

| Criterion | Points |
|---|:---:|
| Correctness of methodology | 3 |
| Interpretation of results | 3 |
| Code quality and organization | 2 |
| **Total** | **8** |

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Grading Details

**Correctness of methodology (3 pts):**

- `my_binom_test` implements all three alternatives correctly and
  matches `scipy.stats.binomtest` on the $p$-value (1 pt).
- All four CI constructions are computed correctly, and the one-sided
  / two-sided equivalence (level $1-\alpha$ vs. $1-2\alpha$) is
  verified numerically (1 pt).
- The coverage simulation is set up correctly (independent samples,
  correct $\geq 0.95$ result, conservative-coverage explanation) (1 pt).
- A/A simulation produces an empirical FPR that is statistically
  indistinguishable from $\alpha$, with a Wilson CI to back it up (1 pt).

**Interpretation of results (3 pts):**

- The four-method CI comparison shows real understanding (not a copy
  of textbook descriptions) --- you explain why Wald is dangerous and
  why Clopper–Pearson is conservative.
- The decision rule based on $\mu^{*}$ is concrete and tied to the
  business case.
- The "what a CI does *not* mean" paragraph contains a real scenario,
  not just a slogan.
- A/A failure modes are realistic and product-specific.

**Code quality and organization (2 pts):**

- `my_binom_test` is a single reusable function used across all
  subsequent sections (not re-implemented inline).
- Plots have axis labels, titles, and legible legends.
- Narrative text precedes every code block.
:::

# Defense {.unnumbered}

During the defense (5–7 minutes) you will be asked *"What if?"*
questions that probe whether you understand **why** each construction
exists, for example:

- *"Your Wilson CI is narrower than your inversion-based CI. Which one
  should you put in the dashboard, and why?"*
- *"The PM says: 'I see a 95% CI of [0.61, 0.69]. Doesn't that mean
  there is a 95% chance the true rate is in that range?' What is your
  one-sentence answer?"*
- *"Your coverage simulation gave 0.962 instead of 0.95. Is that a bug,
  or expected? How would you tell the difference?"*
- *"Your A/A test gave an empirical FPR of 0.07 instead of 0.05. Is
  the difference statistically meaningful, or is it just simulation
  noise? Show me the calculation."*
- *"Switch your test from one-sided to two-sided in your code right
  now. By how much does the lower CI bound move, and does that change
  your recommendation?"*
- *"Set $N$ to half of your Lab 1 plan and re-run the CI. What does
  the new width tell you about whether the test is actually
  decisive?"*

You may also be asked to **change one parameter live** in your code
and explain the resulting shift.
